ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a broad class for canned fruit food products (FOODON_03301710); no exact term for canned apricot in syrup exists. closest_parent match applied as this is the nearest defensible ancestor class.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for canned apricot in syrup; not clinically coded as a distinct entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of canned apricot in syrup is approximately 120 g (about half a cup drained), providing roughly 100 kcal, predominantly from added sugar in the syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Canned Apricot in Syrup — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for canned apricot in syrup (cr_id F04030010). All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for canned apricot in heavy syrup found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 64 is derived from the closest analogous entry: canned apricots in light syrup (GI ~64, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Heavy syrup increases sugar load but the GI value is expected to be similar or marginally higher; 64 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 25.76) / 100 ≈ 16.5, rounded to 16.4 using 21.47 g carb per 100g × 1.2 serving = 25.76 g carb per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (19.87 g/100g) from heavy syrup drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite very low sodium content (4 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid syrup-packed canned fruit; choose water-packed or juice-packed apricots to reduce added sugar by approximately 40%, thereby limiting insulin-mediated sodium retention as described in AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 19.87 g/100g with dietary fibre only 1.6 g/100g and medium GI of 64 — high glycaemic load per serving (≈16) indicates rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned apricot in heavy syrup is not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its very high added sugar content and medium-high glycaemic load; if consumed, limit to a 50 g portion and pair with a protein source such as plain yogurt to attenuate the postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (19.87 g/100g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and de novo lipogenesis; fat content is negligible (0.08 g/100g) and sodium is very low (4 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high added sugar from heavy syrup poses a cardiovascular risk through elevated triglycerides and insulin resistance; individuals with CVD should select water-packed or fresh apricots and limit portion size to reduce sugar-driven cardiovascular burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 19.87 g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators and advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation, overriding the anti-inflammatory carotenoid content of apricot
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid syrup-packed canned apricots and choose fresh or water-packed alternatives to retain beta-carotene benefits without the inflammatory burden of high added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned apricot in syrup is a commercially processed fruit product consisting of whole or halved apricot pieces preserved in heavy sugar syrup, typically in a sealed tin or glass jar. The apricots are golden-orange in colour with a soft, tender texture and a sweet, mildly tangy flavour characteristic of stone fruit. The canning process involves blanching the fruit, packing it in a sucrose-based syrup solution, and heat-sterilising the sealed container to extend shelf life. Per 100 g, the product provides approximately 83 kcal, 21.47 g carbohydrate (of which 19.87 g is sugar from the added syrup), 0.53 g protein, 0.08 g fat, and 1.6 g dietary fibre. Sodium is very low at 4 mg/100g, making it suitable for low-sodium diets in isolation, but the high added sugar content (nearly 20 g/100g) raises significant concerns for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at 64 (medium) with a glycaemic load of approximately 16 per 120 g serving, indicating a meaningful postprandial glucose response. Clinically, the heavy syrup substantially elevates the glycaemic load compared to fresh or water-packed apricots. Canned apricots in syrup are widely available globally and are used as a dessert topping, in baked goods, or consumed as a snack. Nutritionally superior alternatives include apricots canned in water or natural juice, or fresh apricots, which retain carotenoid content without the added sugar burden.
common_user_queries: Is canned apricot in syrup healthy?
Can diabetics eat canned apricot in syrup?
How much sugar is in canned apricot?
Is tinned apricot good for high blood pressure?
Difference between apricot in syrup and apricot in juice

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F04030010) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (canned apricots in light syrup, Atkinson et al. 2008), clearly documented. All hard gates satisfied.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed as negligible/trace per HPB source; fat_g is 0.08 g/100g (not zero). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this product as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived fat. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on canned apricots in light syrup (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI entry for heavy-syrup variant found in the University of Sydney GI Database. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. foodon_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as FOODON_03301710 (canned fruit food product) is the nearest defensible ancestor class in FoodOn; no exact FoodOn term for canned apricot in syrup exists.
